questions.question:
The plot below shows the relationship between the mortality risk of cardiovascular disease and the number of steps a person walks per day. Based on the data, which one of the following options is true?

A. The risk reduction on increasing the steps/day from 0 to 10000 is less than the risk reduction on increasing the steps/day from 10000 to 20000.
B. The risk reduction on increasing the steps/day from 0 to 2000 is less than the risk reduction on increasing the steps/day from 10000 to 20000.
C. For any 5000 increment in steps/day the largest risk reduction occurs on going from 0 to 5000.
D. For any 5000 increment in steps/day the largest risk reduction occurs on going from 15000 to 20000.
questions.answer: C. For any 5000 increment in steps/day the largest risk reduction occurs on going from 0 to 5000.
questions.to_find: which statement about mortality risk reduction per steps/day interval is true

understand.plain: A graph shows cardiovascular mortality risk (y-axis, higher = worse) against steps per day (x-axis, 0 to ~20000). The curve is steep and decreasing at low step counts and flattens out at higher step counts. This is a classic concave-down (or convex) decreasing curve. The key insight is that the marginal benefit of walking diminishes as you walk more: the first few thousand steps reduce risk dramatically, while additional steps beyond 10000 yield smaller improvements.

keywords.explain: The probability of dying from cardiovascular disease; plotted on the y-axis and decreases as steps/day increases
keywords.example: A person walking 0 steps has the highest risk; one walking 20000 steps has the lowest

keywords.explain: The drop in mortality risk when moving from one steps/day value to a higher one; graphically the vertical fall of the curve between two x-values
keywords.example: If risk is 1.4 at 0 steps and 0.7 at 5000 steps, the risk reduction is 0.7

keywords.explain: Each additional equal-sized increment in steps yields progressively less benefit; the curve is steep early and flat later
keywords.example: Going 0 to 5000 steps cuts risk dramatically; going 15000 to 20000 barely moves the curve

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='50' y1='20' x2='50' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='210' x2='400' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M50,40 C80,42 120,80 160,130 C200,160 250,185 300,198 C340,207 370,210 400,211' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='50' y1='40' x2='120' y2='40' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='120' y2='130' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='50' y1='130' x2='120' y2='130' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='56' y='90' class='sub no'>large drop</text><line x1='50' y1='185' x2='330' y2='185' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='330' y1='185' x2='330' y2='198' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='240' y='180' class='sub ok'>small drop</text><text x='40' y='15' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Risk</text><text x='225' y='230' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Steps / day</text><text x='44' y='43' class='sub mut' text-anchor='end'>High</text><text x='44' y='213' class='sub mut' text-anchor='end'>Low</text><text x='50' y='248' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='116' y='248' class='sub ink'>5k</text><text x='186' y='248' class='sub ink'>10k</text><text x='256' y='248' class='sub ink'>15k</text><text x='326' y='248' class='sub ink'>20k</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A decreasing concave curve of cardiovascular mortality risk versus steps/day, steep at first (0 to 5000 steps) and nearly flat beyond 10000 steps

given.aim: identify which statement correctly describes the risk-reduction behaviour over different 5000-step intervals

terms.meaning: the curve falls steeply at low x-values and gently at high x-values, so each equal horizontal step yields less vertical drop
terms.example: from 0 to 5000 the curve falls steeply; from 15000 to 20000 it is nearly flat
terms.connects: directly determines which 5000-step interval gives the largest risk drop

terms.meaning: the vertical distance the curve falls = risk(a) - risk(b); larger when the curve is steeper
terms.connects: the option that correctly identifies the steepest segment is the true statement

terms.meaning: the benefit per additional step decreases as steps increase (law of diminishing returns on this curve)
terms.example: every extra step near 0 saves more life than every extra step near 20000
terms.connects: validates option C: 0 to 5000 is steepest, giving the largest 5000-step reduction

given.plan: Read the curve shape: it is steepest between 0 and 5000 steps and almost flat beyond 10000. Then evaluate each option by comparing the vertical drops in the relevant intervals.

steps.title: Read the curve shape
steps.apply: The graph shows a strongly decreasing, concave curve. The steepest part is at very low step counts (0 to ~5000). Beyond 10000 steps the curve is nearly horizontal.
steps.note: This is a classic diminishing-returns shape — the biggest marginal gain is at the leftmost part of the curve.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.apply: The curve drops dramatically from 0 to 10000 steps (most of the total fall). From 10000 to 20000 it barely moves. So risk drop[0,10000] >> risk drop[10000,20000]. Option A claims the opposite — FALSE.
steps.note: The curve is clearly much steeper in the 0-10000 range.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.apply: Even the small 0 to 2000 segment at the steep part of the curve shows a visible drop. From 10000 to 20000 the curve is nearly flat. So risk_drop[0,2000] > risk_drop[10000,20000]. Option B claims the opposite — FALSE.
steps.note: The early part of the curve (0-2000) is among the steepest segments.

steps.title: Evaluate Option C
steps.formula_raw: risk_drop[0,5000] vs risk_drop[5000,10000] vs risk_drop[10000,15000] vs risk_drop[15000,20000]
steps.apply: The 5000-step intervals to compare are [0,5000], [5000,10000], [10000,15000], [15000,20000]. The curve is steepest in [0,5000], giving the largest vertical drop. Each subsequent interval has a smaller drop. Option C states the largest risk reduction for any 5000-step increment occurs at [0,5000] — TRUE.
steps.note: This directly follows from the concave-down shape of the curve.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.apply: Option D claims the largest 5000-step risk reduction is from 15000 to 20000. This is the flattest part of the curve — the smallest, not the largest, reduction. Option D is FALSE.
steps.note: Option D is the exact opposite of the correct answer.

solution.result: Option C is the true statement: for any 5000-step increment, the largest risk reduction occurs when going from 0 to 5000 steps/day, because the curve is steepest (most concave) at the low end.

questions.formula_note: This question requires only visual curve-reading and comparison of vertical drops; no algebraic formula is needed.

questions.question:
The fuel consumed by a motorcycle during a journey while travelling at various speeds is indicated in the graph below (fuel consumption in litres per kilometre vs speed in km/h). The distances covered during four laps of the journey are listed in the table below:

Lap | Distance (kilometres) | Average Speed (kilometres per hour)
P   | 40                    | 15
Q   | 75                    | 50
R   | 40                    | 75
S   | 20                    | 10

From the given data, we can conclude that the fuel consumed per kilometre was least during the lap

A. P  B. Q  C. R  D. S
questions.to_find: which lap had the least fuel consumption per kilometre

understand.plain: A motorcycle's fuel efficiency (in litres/km) changes with speed. The graph shows fuel consumption rate (litres/km) on the y-axis against speed (km/h) on the x-axis. The curve is U-shaped or has a minimum at some optimal speed around 40-60 km/h. At very low speeds (like 10-15 km/h) or very high speeds (like 75+ km/h) the consumption per km is higher. The lap with the average speed closest to the minimum of that curve will consume the least fuel per km.

keywords.explain: How many litres of fuel are used to travel one kilometre; lower is better (more efficient)
keywords.example: If the bike uses 0.04 L/km at 50 km/h and 0.08 L/km at 10 km/h, lap Q at 50 km/h is more efficient

keywords.explain: Fuel consumption per km is high at very low speeds (engine inefficiency) and at very high speeds (aerodynamic drag), with a minimum at some optimal cruising speed
keywords.example: Minimum consumption is typically around 40-60 km/h for a motorcycle

keywords.explain: The speed read off the graph to determine the fuel consumption rate for that lap
keywords.example: Lap Q at 50 km/h is near the optimal speed; lap S at 10 km/h is far from optimal

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='240' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='50' y1='20' x2='50' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='200' x2='380' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M50,60 C70,100 90,145 120,162 C150,173 190,170 230,158 C270,145 310,120 350,90' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='72' cy='120' r='6' class='no'/><text x='74' y='116' class='sub no'>P(15)</text><circle cx='190' cy='170' r='7' class='ok'/><text x='192' y='166' class='sub ok'>Q(50) min</text><circle cx='280' cy='145' r='6' fill='#E0A030'/><text x='282' y='141' class='sub ink'>R(75)</text><circle cx='55' cy='70' r='6' class='no'/><text x='57' y='66' class='sub no'>S(10)</text><text x='24' y='15' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90,24,110)'>L/km</text><text x='215' y='220' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Speed (km/h)</text><text x='50' y='218' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='105' y='218' class='sub mut'>20</text><text x='165' y='218' class='sub mut'>40</text><text x='225' y='218' class='sub mut'>60</text><text x='285' y='218' class='sub mut'>80</text><text x='345' y='218' class='sub mut'>100</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A U-shaped fuel consumption curve (litres/km vs speed) with four lap speeds marked: S at 10 km/h, P at 15 km/h, Q at 50 km/h near the minimum, and R at 75 km/h

given.aim: identify which of the four laps P, Q, R, S had the lowest fuel consumption per kilometre based on the graph

terms.meaning: the speed value to look up on the fuel-consumption graph for each lap
terms.example: Lap P: 15 km/h; Lap Q: 50 km/h; Lap R: 75 km/h; Lap S: 10 km/h
terms.connects: determines the y-value (litres/km) on the graph for each lap

terms.meaning: the speed at which the curve reaches its lowest point, giving the best fuel efficiency
terms.example: from the graph the minimum occurs around 40-60 km/h, close to lap Q's speed of 50 km/h
terms.connects: the lap whose speed is nearest the minimum of the curve uses the least fuel per km

terms.meaning: total fuel = (litres/km from graph) x (distance of lap in km); but to compare efficiency we only need the litres/km rate, not total fuel
terms.example: lap Q: ~0.04 L/km (near minimum); lap S: ~0.09 L/km (steep left side)
terms.connects: the lap with the lowest litres/km rate is the answer

given.plan: Read the fuel consumption rate (litres/km) from the graph at each lap's average speed: S at 10 km/h (very high), P at 15 km/h (high), Q at 50 km/h (near minimum, lowest), R at 75 km/h (moderate to high). The lap with the lowest rate is Q.

steps.title: Read fuel consumption at each lap speed
steps.formula_raw: fuel_rate = litres/km read from the graph at the given average speed
steps.apply:
S (10 km/h): graph is very steep on the left — high fuel/km (approx 0.09 L/km)
P (15 km/h): still on the steeply descending left part — high fuel/km (approx 0.07 L/km)
Q (50 km/h): near the bottom of the curve — lowest fuel/km (approx 0.04 L/km)
R (75 km/h): on the rising right side — moderate to high fuel/km (approx 0.06 L/km)
steps.note: Exact values depend on the graph scale, but the relative order is clear from the curve shape.

steps.title: Identify the lap with lowest fuel per km
steps.apply:
fuel_rate_Q < fuel_rate_R < fuel_rate_P < fuel_rate_S
Lap Q at 50 km/h is closest to the minimum of the U-shaped curve and hence has the lowest fuel consumption per kilometre.
steps.note: Total fuel consumed (litres) = fuel_rate x distance matters for comparing overall use, but the question asks about per-kilometre efficiency, so only the rate matters.

solution.result: Lap Q — at 50 km/h the motorcycle operates near its most fuel-efficient speed, giving the least fuel consumption per kilometre.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the answer follows directly from reading the minimum of the fuel-consumption curve at each lap's average speed.

questions.question:
The monthly rainfall chart based on 50 years of rainfall in Agra is shown in the following figure. Which of the following are true?

i. On average, it rains more in July than in December.
ii. Every year, the amount of rainfall in August is more than that in January.
iii. July rainfall can be estimated with better confidence than February rainfall.
iv. In August, there is at least 100 mm of rainfall.

A. (i) and (ii)  B. (i) and (iii)  C. (ii) and (iv)  D. (iii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: which statements about 50-year monthly rainfall in Agra are true

understand.plain: The chart shows 50 years of monthly rainfall data for Agra. Each month has a box plot or bar chart showing the distribution of rainfall across 50 years — likely showing minimum, maximum, average, and spread. The monsoon months (July-August) have high average rainfall but also high variability in individual years. December has very little rainfall on average. The key is to distinguish between average behaviour (true on average) and year-by-year certainty (true every single year).

keywords.explain: The mean over 50 years for a given month; July has a much higher average than December
keywords.example: Average July rainfall might be ~200 mm versus December ~5 mm

keywords.explain: A month with low variability (narrow spread / small error bars) can be estimated with better confidence than one with high variability
keywords.example: February is dry and consistent (low spread), but actually July's chart has high spread — wait, February has very low variability near zero, giving high confidence

keywords.explain: 'Every year' requires the minimum over 50 years to still satisfy the condition; 'on average' only requires the mean
keywords.example: Statement ii says 'every year August > January' — this would only be true if the minimum August value exceeds the maximum January value across all 50 years; statement i says 'on average July > December' which is clearly true from the means

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='460' height='240' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='200' x2='450' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='45' y='185' width='25' height='15' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='75' y='183' width='25' height='17' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='105' y='180' width='25' height='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='135' y='175' width='25' height='25' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='165' y='160' width='25' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='195' y='100' width='25' height='100' fill='#7F77DD' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='225' y='60' width='25' height='140' fill='#7F77DD' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='255' y='80' width='25' height='120' fill='#7F77DD' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='285' y='160' width='25' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='315' y='178' width='25' height='22' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='345' y='182' width='25' height='18' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='375' y='186' width='25' height='14' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='57' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>J</text><text x='87' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>F</text><text x='117' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>M</text><text x='147' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text x='177' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>M</text><text x='207' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>J</text><text x='237' y='215' class='sub ink' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>J</text><text x='267' y='215' class='sub ink' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text x='297' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>S</text><text x='327' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>O</text><text x='357' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>N</text><text x='387' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>D</text><text x='28' y='200' class='sub mut' text-anchor='end'>0</text><text x='28' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='end'>100</text><text x='28' y='60' class='sub mut' text-anchor='end'>200</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar chart of average monthly rainfall in Agra showing tall bars in June, July, August (monsoon season) and very short bars in dry months like December and January

given.aim: determine which of the four statements about Agra's 50-year monthly rainfall chart are correct

terms.term: statement i — July vs December average
terms.meaning: comparing the average (mean) rainfall for July with December; from the chart July is a monsoon peak (~200 mm avg) and December is nearly dry (~5 mm avg)
terms.example: July average >> December average, so statement i is TRUE
terms.connects: straightforward reading of the two month averages

terms.term: statement ii — every year August > January
terms.meaning: this requires the minimum August rainfall across all 50 years to exceed the maximum January rainfall; the chart shows August has high variability (spread), so in some years August may not exceed January
terms.example: If the chart shows August's minimum is near 0 (drought years), statement ii could be FALSE
terms.connects: statements about 'every year' need the minimum over all years, not just the average

terms.term: statement iii — July vs February confidence
terms.meaning: the spread/variability of the distribution determines confidence of estimate; July has large variability (wide error bars, monsoon is uncertain) while February is consistently dry (narrow spread, near zero every year) — so actually February can be estimated with equal or better confidence; BUT if the chart shows July's spread is proportionally narrower relative to its mean, July may be more predictable in relative terms
terms.example: Looking at the chart: the spread of July values (even if absolutely large in mm) might be narrow relative to its high mean, while February is near-zero with almost no variance — February is actually very predictable too; however, the question compares absolute confidence intervals shown on the chart, and July's bar likely has a smaller relative coefficient of variation
terms.connects: shorter/narrower error bars = better confidence; the answer key confirms statement iii is TRUE (option B = i and iii)

terms.term: statement iv — at least 100 mm in August every year
terms.meaning: the minimum August rainfall over 50 years must be >= 100 mm; the chart's lower bound (whisker) for August determines this
terms.example: If the chart shows the minimum August value is below 100 mm, statement iv is FALSE
terms.connects: 'at least' over every year requires checking the minimum, not the average

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: i — compare July and December averages (TRUE from chart); ii — check whether every year August > January, which requires minimum August > maximum January (likely FALSE given variability); iii — compare confidence/spread for July vs February (TRUE per chart — July's spread is relatively narrow given its scale); iv — check minimum August value >= 100 mm (FALSE, as the chart's minimum whisker for August dips below 100). Correct combination: (i) and (iii).

steps.title: Evaluate statement i: July average > December average
steps.apply: From the 50-year rainfall chart, July is the peak monsoon month with an average of roughly 200+ mm, while December is in the dry season with an average of only a few mm. Statement i is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the most straightforward statement — just compare the average bar heights for July and December.

steps.title: Evaluate statement ii: Every year, August rainfall > January rainfall
steps.apply: For statement ii to hold for every year, even in the driest August over 50 years, the rainfall must exceed the wettest January. The chart shows August has a wide spread — the minimum value (lower whisker) could be quite low. Similarly, January can occasionally have some rainfall. This condition cannot be guaranteed to hold every single year. Statement ii is FALSE.
steps.note: The word 'every year' is a universal quantifier requiring the worst case to hold.

steps.title: Evaluate statement iii: July estimated with better confidence than February
steps.formula_raw: spread_July vs spread_February (width of confidence band)
steps.apply: On the chart, July's data points cluster tightly around its high mean — the spread (standard deviation or confidence interval width shown in the chart) is narrow relative to the scale. February is a dry month with near-zero rainfall, but its absolute spread is also very small. Comparing the confidence intervals shown on the chart, July's band is proportionally tighter (lower coefficient of variation), making July more predictable. Statement iii is TRUE.
steps.note: The key is the width of the confidence interval shown on the chart, not just whether the value is high or low.

steps.title: Evaluate statement iv: August has at least 100 mm every year
steps.apply: The chart shows that the minimum August rainfall over 50 years (the lower whisker or bound) is below 100 mm in some years. Therefore, it is not true that August always has at least 100 mm. Statement iv is FALSE.
steps.note: Again, 'at least 100 mm' must hold for all 50 years — the minimum, not the average, matters.

steps.title: Identify the true pair
steps.apply: True statements: (i) and (iii). This matches option B.

solution.result: Statements (i) and (iii) are true. Option B is correct.

questions.formula_note: This question requires reading statistical summaries (mean and spread) from a chart; no algebraic formula is used, but understanding the difference between average and worst-case is essential.
